content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 6, 2016

Kelso Barnett
328 First Street East
Sonoma, CA 95476

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-032

Dear Mr. Barnett:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Additionally, the Commission does not advise about past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8).) Therefore, nothing in this advice letter should be construed to advise about any conduct that has already taken place.

QUESTIONS

1. Were your past actions involving a major mixed-use real estate development project in Sonoma – including speaking out against it in the press, creating a website opposing it, and speaking at a Planning Commission meeting about the project's impact on your home and neighborhood – permissible under the Act?

2. May you speak at an upcoming Sonoma County Planning Commission ("Planning Commission") meeting about the project if the decision regarding the project does not appear on the agenda?

3. May you submit written communication about the project via "late mail," or written comments delivered to the Planning Commission members by the City's planning director as part of the public agenda packet?

________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-032
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. This question relates to past conduct and the Fair Political Practices Commission
("FPPC") does not provide advice regarding past conduct. However, we have
provided some guidance below regarding the Act's conflict of interest rules that may
be of assistance to you in the future.

2. & 3. Yes. You may appear before the Planning Commission and comment on the project
even if it does not appear on the agenda and you may submit written comments about the
development via "late mail," so long as you limit your comments to the project's impact on
your personal residence. Your appearances or comments before the Planning Commission
may be permissible under a narrow exception to the definition of "influencing" under the
Act, as described below. Please note that shared staffing between the Planning Commission
and the Preservation Commission means that meeting or otherwise communicating with
shared staff outside the exception would be an appearance or contact with your own agency
for the purpose of influencing a governmental decision.

FACTS

You currently serve on the City of Sonoma Design Review & Historic Preservation
Commission ("Preservation Commission"). The Preservation Commission's focus is on historic
preservation and aesthetic design of projects including signage, color, and architectural design. The
Preservation Commission's decisions may be appealed to the City Council.

A major mixed-use real estate development project has been proposed within 500 feet of
your residence. The project must go through an approval process before the Planning Commission
before it reaches the Preservation Commission. Generally, a project begins with a study session to
get initial feedback from the Planning Commission and the community. The developers then submit
an official project. The Planning Commission holds hearings and if the project is approved, then the
Preservation Commission considers it.

The Preservation Commission considers the aesthetics of the project such as colors, the
façade, landscaping and any potential demolition of historic structures. The Preservation
Commission's decisions can be appealed to the City Council, which has final decision-making
authority.

If the project reaches the Preservation Commission, you will recuse yourself from the
decision due to the project's proximity to your home.

The Preservation Commission has no authority over the City Council or the Planning
Commission. However, the Preservation Commission shares staff with the Planning Commission,
which has three employees in the Planning Department. The Planning Department prepares project
submittals for both the Planning Commission and the Preservation Commission.

Currently, the development project is in the "study session" stage before the Planning
Commission and the Planning Director is "in charge of" and oversees the process. The developers

File No. A-16-032
Page No. 3

may submit an official project later this year. If development receives further approval, an
Environmental Impact Report will likely be necessary, which will probably take an additional year.
The Planning Commission will hold more hearings and will ultimately approve or deny the
development of the project. If the Planning Commission approves the project, it will go before the
Preservation Commission. You anticipate the process may take two to three years.

You helped to develop an informational website about the project, encouraging the public to
attend the first Planning Commission study session and alerting the public to serious concerns about
the development. Your name and position with the Preservation Commission does not appear on the
website. You were also quoted in one newspaper article about the project as saying the
neighborhood "is not against development, we just want a development to fit the neighborhood."

You also spoke at the Planning Commission meeting about the project, did not identify
yourself as a Design Review Commissioner, and commented about the project's potential effect on
your residence and neighborhood. You also played a video showing a 3D rendering that showed the
mass and scale of the project as it relates to the surrounding area.

You have not spoken to any city council member or planning commissioner about this
project individually or outside a public hearing.

You wish to know whether your past actions involving the project were permissible under
the Act.

In addition, you wish to obtain advice about whether you may speak at an upcoming
Planning Commission meeting about the project even if it does not appear on the agenda, and
whether you may submit written communication about the project via "late mail," or comments
delivered to the commissioners by the city planning director as part of the public agenda packet.
"Late mail" consists of public comments submitted to the Planning Commission after it has posted
public comments on its website, usually the Friday before a commission meeting.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section
87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Section 87103 defines interest from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes any
real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).)

Based on the information you provided, you have a financial interest in your personal
residence. Because your home is within 500 feet of the proposed project, you will be recusing
yourself from decisions involving the development.

Your questions generally revolve around whether you will be making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision if you contact another city employee or appear

File No. A-16-032
Page No. 4

before the Planning Commission regarding the proposed development project, assuming it is an
otherwise disqualifying conflict of interest.

We therefore limit our analysis to Step Four of our conflicts analysis – the determination of
whether you will be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision when
you appear before the Planning Commission or communicate with other city employees.

Step Four: Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only when a public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his [or her] official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to know he [or she] has a financial
interest." (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).)

In other words, an official is not prohibited from contacting an agency under the Act's
conflict of interest provisions if the official is not making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision. The Commission has defined "making," "participating in making," and
"influencing" a governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.)

Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "makes a governmental decision" when
the official, "official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his
or her agency." (Section 87100; Regulation 18704(a).)

Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates in a
governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the
purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review." (Section
87100; Regulation 18704(b).)

Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules that address whether a public
official is using or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before the public
official's own agency or "an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her
agency for the purpose of affecting a decision." In these cases, if the official "contacts or appears
before" or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency, then he or she is attempting to influence a governmental decision. (Regulation
18704(c)(1).)

The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an agency other
than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control
of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(2).) Under this rule, the official cannot act or
purpose "to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact"
to influence a decision that will have a material financial effect on his or her economic interests.

File No. A-16-032
Page No. 5

Appearances before the Planning Commission:

Your questions generally pertain to whether you, as a member of the City's Preservation
Commission, may appear before the Planning Commission, or contact Planning Commission or its
employees regarding the proposed development project. The threshold determination must be made
regarding whether you are using your position to influence your own agency, or an agency other
than your own agency, in communicating with, or making the appearances before the Planning
Commission or its employees.

In this regard, we previously advised in the Farrell Advice Letter, No. I-03-121:

"[I]t is important to realize that staff does not exist in a vacuum; it is not a generic
entity with its own identity. Rather, staff is a component of the agency to which it is
assigned. An 'agency' includes the staff assigned exclusively to a public official's
agency (Larmore Advice Letter, No. A-00-275; Martello Advice Letter, No. A-85-190)
as well as staff that are shared between that and another agency (West Advice Letter,
No. A-88-413)."

In the past, for instance, we have advised that a member of a design review board could not
meet or communicate with a staff member of another city department on behalf a client if: (1) the
staff member is assigned or involved in a project, analysis, presentation, or other matter that is or
will be brought before the design review board; and (2) it is reasonably foreseeable that the design
review board's decision regarding the item in which the staff member is involved will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the member's economic interests.

In short, when staff is shared for a project which is, or will be, before the official's agency,
meeting or otherwise communicating with the shared staff regarding the project is an appearance or
communication with the official's own agency. (Farrell Advice Letter, supra, emphasis added.)

Applied to your facts, as a member of the Preservation Commission, you may not appear
before or communicate with a staff member of another city department, including the Planning
Commission, regarding the development project, if the staff member assigned to or involved in the
project, may potentially appear before your own agency. Such contacts would be considered an
appearance or communication before the Preservation Commission. This includes, but is not limited
to appearances before or communications with a staff member² who advises or provides analysis to
the Planning Commission regarding the development project, and it is reasonably foreseeable that
the Planning Commission's decision regarding the project in which the staff member is involved
will have a material financial effect on your economic interests.

Exceptions:

Regulation 18704 includes an exception to "making, participating in, or influencing a
governmental decision" that specifically authorizes "appearances as a member of the general
public." (Regulation 18704(d)(2).)

² This includes the Planning Director, associate or senior planners and support staff that are shared between the
Planning Commission and the Preservation Commission.

File No. A-16-032
Page No. 6

This narrow exception includes appearances by an official before his or her "agency in the
course of its prescribed governmental function if the official is appearing on matters related solely
to his or her personal interests," including interests in real property owned entirely by the official
and members of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18704(d)(2)(A), emphasis added.)

Such an appearance, when properly made, does not constitute making, participating in
making, or influencing a governmental decision. However, this rule must be narrowly construed as
dictated by the fundamental rules of statutory construction.³

Therefore, if you use this exception to appear before the Planning Commission, you must
limit your comments to your personal interests (your residence), and you should make clear that
you are not speaking in the interest of any other person or group, and that you are not acting in any
official capacity. (Burns Advice Letter, No. A-06-178, citing to McHugh Advice Letter, No. 1-98-
324; Gallagher Advice Letter, No. 1-94-279; and Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151.) Similarly,
you may not discuss your support or opposition to the development decision with other
Commissioners, shared staff, or consultants outside of any public meetings. (Simonian Advice
Letter, No. A-08-096; Burns Advice Letter, No. A-06-178, citing McHugh Advice Letter, No. 1-98-
324.)

Accordingly, you may appear before the Planning Commission and comment on the
project even if it does not appear on the agenda, and you may submit written comments about the
project via "late mail," so long as you limit your comments to your personal interests, such as the development's impact on your personal residence.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ER:jgl

³ Julius Goldman's Egg City v. Air Pollution Control Dept. of Ventura County (1981) 116 Cal.App.3d
741, which provides that an exception to a general rule must be narrowly construed. See also Estate of Banerjee (1978)
21 Cal.3d 527; Valdez v. Federal Mut Ins Co. (1969) 272 Cal.App.2d 223.

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 8100l(b).) Specifically, Section 
87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or 
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 defines interest from which a conflict of interest may arise and includes any 
real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
Based on the information you provided, you have a financial interest in your personal 
residence. Because your home is within 500 feet of the proposed project, you will be recusing 
yourself from decisions involving the development. 
Your questions generally revolve around whether you will be making, participating in 
making, or influencing a governmental decision if you contact another city employee or appear

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only when a public official "make[s],

governmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to know he [or she] has a financial 
interest." (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) 
In other words, an official is not prohibited from contacting an agency under the Act's 
conflict of interest provisions if the official is not making, participating in making, or influencing a 
governmental decision. The Commission has defined "making," "participating in making," and 
"influencing" a governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.)

the official, "official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits 
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his 
or her agency." (Section 87100; Regulation l 8704(a).) 
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates in a 
governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the 
purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review." (Section 
87100; Regulation 18704(b ). )

decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before the public

than your own agency, in communicating with, or making the appearances before the Planning

entity with its own identity. Rather, staff is a component of the agency to which it is 
assigned. An 'agency' includes the staff assigned exclusively to a public official's 
agency (Larmore Advice Letter, No. A-00-275; Martello Advice Letter, No. A-85-190) 
as well as staff that are shared between that and another agency (West Advice Letter, 
No. A-88-413)."

324; Gallagher Advice Letter, No. 1-94-279; and Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151.) Similarly,

Commissioners, shared staff, or consultants outside of any public meetings. (Simonian Advice 
Letter, No. A-08-096; Burns Advice Letter, No. A-06-178, citing McHugh Advice Letter, No. 1-98-
324.) 
Accordingly, you may appear before the Planning Commission and comment on the 
project even if it does not appear on the agenda, and you may submit written comments about 
the project via "late mail," so long as you limit your comments to your personal interests, 
such as the development's impact on your personal residence.
